edges.description: Localized gingival or alveolar-ridge proliferation produces the clinically apparent oral mass.
Larger oral masses can block latch or oral intake, causing neonatal feeding difficulty.
Very large lesions can threaten airway patency, although not all cases produce respiratory distress.

nodes.description: Congenital epulis is a localized benign congenital lesion of gingival or alveolar-ridge soft tissue. Current pathology literature favors a non-neural, likely mesenchymal or hamartomatous origin, although the exact histogenesis remains uncertain. A hypothesized intrauterine hormonal influence has been proposed to explain the strong female predilection and postnatal growth arrest, but it remains unproven. Local granular-cell proliferation forms the exophytic neonatal oral mass and can mechanically impair feeding or, rarely, respiration.

evidence.reference_title: Obstructive congenital gingival granular cell tumour.
evidence.snippet: Congenital gingival granular cell tumours (CGCT) are rare and always benign intraoral tumours originating from the alveolar ridge.

evidence.reference_title: Cytoplasmic Granules in Granular Cell Tumor and Congenital Epulis: A Histochemical and Immunohistochemical Note.
evidence.snippet: CE, however, lacks S-100 expression and demonstrates absent or weak CD68 staining, favoring a non-neural, likely mesenchymal or hamartomatous origin.

nodes.description: Large oral lesions may interfere with mouth closure, latch, or normal neonatal feeding.

evidence.reference_title: Large Congenital Epulis: A Neonatal Tumour with Striking Appearance, but Simple Management.
evidence.snippet: Mouth closing and normal feeding were hampered.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital granular cell epulis in a neonate: a case report and review of diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis.
evidence.snippet: a smooth, multilobulated mass on the right maxillary alveolar ridge causing feeding difficulties.

nodes.description: A congenital gingival or alveolar-ridge oral mass is the defining clinical presentation, often pedunculated and protruding into the oral cavity.

evidence.reference_title: Obstructive congenital gingival granular cell tumour.
evidence.snippet: They are typically seen as a mass protruding out of a newborn child's mouth.
It occurs on the gingiva of the anterior alveolar ridge of the jaws.

nodes.description: Local excision of the mass is the main treatment when the lesion interferes with feeding or threatens the airway. Wide destructive margins are generally not implied by the available case literature.

evidence.reference_title: Large Congenital Epulis: A Neonatal Tumour with Striking Appearance, but Simple Management.
evidence.snippet: The mass was excised surgically and baby improved.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital granular cell epulis in a neonate: a case report and review of diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis.
evidence.snippet: Early diagnosis and surgical treatment of CGCE are essential to avoid functional impairment.

nodes.description: Prenatally detected large oral masses may require multidisciplinary delivery planning, airway readiness, and temporary feeding support until definitive excision.

evidence.reference_title: Obstructive congenital epulis: prenatal diagnosis and perinatal management.
evidence.snippet: A multidisciplinary approach to antenatally identified congenital intraoral masses facilitates care at birth.

evidence.reference_title: Obstructive congenital epulis: prenatal diagnosis and perinatal management.
evidence.snippet: Feeding was started early, and both infants were discharged after brief hospital stays.

nodes.description: Large congenital epulis lesions can threaten airway patency, but airway compromise is conditional on lesion size and position rather than an obligate manifestation.

evidence.reference_title: Large Congenital Epulis: A Neonatal Tumour with Striking Appearance, but Simple Management.
evidence.snippet: Larger lesions interfere with mouth closing and normal feeding and may obstruct airways.

evidence.reference_title: Obstructive congenital epulis: prenatal diagnosis and perinatal management.
evidence.snippet: concerns regarding the patency of the airway at birth necessitated development of a multidisciplinary team
